name: Allergic Rhinitis

key_questions: Seasonal (tree/grass/ragweed) or perennial (dust mites, pet dander, mold)?
Duration and timing of symptoms — morning vs throughout day?
Current medications including nasal decongestants (rhinitis medicamentosa risk)?
red_flags: Unilateral nasal obstruction or bleeding → polyp or malignancy
Facial pain/pressure + fever + purulent discharge → sinusitis
Symptoms not responding to adequate trial of intranasal corticosteroids

first_line.name: Intranasal Corticosteroid (INCS)
first_line.drugs: Fluticasone propionate (Flonase) 50 mcg/spray: 2 sprays each nostril OD | Fluticasone furoate (Flonase Sensimist) 27.5 mcg/spray: 2 sprays each nostril OD | Mometasone (Nasonex) 50 mcg/spray: 2 sprays each nostril OD (OTC since 2022) | Budesonide (Rhinocort) 2 sprays each nostril OD | Ciclesonide (Omnaris) 200 mcg/day (2 sprays each nostril OD)
first_line.notes: Most effective pharmacotherapy for allergic rhinitis — superior to antihistamines for congestion and nasal symptoms. Takes 1–2 weeks for full effect. Spray toward outer ear, not nasal septum (prevents septum perforation). Can be used long-term. Note: Flonase = fluticasone propionate 50 mcg/spray; Flonase Sensimist = fluticasone furoate 27.5 mcg/spray — different molecules and strengths.
first_line.preferred_when: Persistent symptoms or predominantly nasal congestion

first_line.name: Oral Non-Sedating Antihistamine (2nd-generation)
first_line.drugs: Cetirizine (Reactine) 10 mg OD | Loratadine (Claritin) 10 mg OD | Fexofenadine (Allegra) 120 mg OD (seasonal AR) | Bilastine (Blexten) 20 mg OD | Rupatadine (Rupall) 10 mg OD | Levocetirizine (Xyzal) 5 mg OD
first_line.notes: First-line for mild-intermittent symptoms or sneezing/itch predominance. Non-sedating (second-generation). Take daily for seasonal allergies (continuous > PRN). Cetirizine: mildly sedating in some — take at night. Bilastine, rupatadine, and levocetirizine are Canadian-available options endorsed by CSACI.
first_line.preferred_when: Itching, sneezing, watery eyes as predominant symptoms

second_line.name: Intranasal Antihistamine + INCS Combination (preferred step-up)
second_line.drugs: Azelastine/fluticasone propionate (Dymista) nasal spray: 1 spray each nostril BID
second_line.notes: ARIA 2020 preferred step-up when INCS or antihistamine monotherapy fails. Combination of intranasal antihistamine (azelastine) and INCS (fluticasone) in one spray. Faster onset than INCS alone. Superior to either component alone for moderate-severe AR.

second_line.name: Intranasal Antihistamine (monotherapy)
second_line.drugs: Azelastine (Astelin) nasal spray 1–2 sprays each nostril BID
second_line.notes: Faster onset than oral antihistamines. Useful for breakthrough symptoms or when INCS insufficient. Step up to Dymista (azelastine/fluticasone combo) if inadequate response.

second_line.name: Oral Decongestant — short-term only (NOT in OCP MA algorithm)
second_line.notes: For acute nasal congestion only — do NOT use >5 days (rebound congestion). Avoid in hypertension, hyperthyroidism, BPH, MAOIs. Behind pharmacy counter (pseudoephedrine). IMPORTANT: Pseudoephedrine is NOT in the OCP Minor Ailments prescribing algorithm for allergic rhinitis — pharmacists should recommend or refer to physician, not prescribe under MA authority.

second_line.name: Montelukast (Singulair) — last resort only (NOT in OCP MA algorithm)
second_line.notes: ARIA 2020 conditionally recommends AGAINST montelukast monotherapy for allergic rhinitis due to Health Canada/FDA Black Box Warning (BBW) for serious neuropsychiatric events (depression, suicidality, aggression, sleep disturbances). Reserve as last resort only when other options have failed and there is a clear indication (e.g., concurrent asthma + AR). IMPORTANT: Montelukast is NOT in the OCP Minor Ailments prescribing algorithm for AR — recommend or refer to physician only, do not prescribe under MA authority.

non_pharm: Allergen avoidance: dust mite covers, HEPA filters, minimize outdoor exposure during high pollen
Nasal saline irrigation (Neti pot, NeilMed) — reduces symptoms and medication need
Monitor local pollen counts (Weather Network pollen forecast)
Close windows during high pollen season, use air conditioning
refer_when: No response to adequate trial of INCS after 4–6 weeks
Suspected nasal polyps (unilateral obstruction, anosmia, recurrent sinusitis)
Concurrent asthma not controlled or worsening despite treatment
Immunotherapy candidacy (subcutaneous SCIT or sublingual SLIT — Oralair, Grastek, Acarizax available in Canada)
Severe uncontrolled symptoms despite adequate combination pharmacotherapy
Suspected sinusitis or anatomical obstruction (deviated septum)

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Allergic Rhinitis. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
CSACI (Canadian Society of Allergy and Clinical Immunology). Allergic Rhinitis — Canadian Clinical Practice Guidelines (2022).
Bernstein JA, et al. The diagnosis and management of rhinitis: An updated practice parameter. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2022;150(6):1286–1341. (AAAAI/ACAAI)
Brozek JL, et al. Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma (ARIA) Guidelines — 2016 revision. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2017;140(4):950–958.
